market.name: ASML Holding N.V. - New York Re

buys.title: 2 Stocks the Entire AI Market Depends On !!
buys.reason: ASML is a Dutch company that manufactures lithography systems, the machines that project circuit patterns onto silicon wafers to create semiconductors. Its extreme ultraviolet (EUV) machines are the only tools on Earth capable of printing today's most advanced chip designs, and no other company makes them. TSMC, Samsung, Intel, and every leading edge chip maker buy from ASML because there is no alternative. EUV is how 3 nanometer chips and 2 nanometer chips exist, and ASML's next generation high NA EUV platform is how the nodes beyond that will be printed. As chips become more complex, more EUV exposures are required per chip, and AI is accelerating this litho intensity even faster than any previous technology cycle. The CEO updated 2026 guidance, increasing the expected revenue range to between 36 billion and 40 billion euros while maintaining gross margin expectations of 51 to 53%. ASML is currently under-shipping demand and cannot build EUV machines fast enough to meet customer demand. By 2027, ASML aims to ship at least 80 tools (up from 44 in the prior year), effectively doubling the chip-making capacity it delivers through EUV alone. Additionally, AI-grade DRAM adoption is accelerating—DRAM now accounts for 51% of net sales systems (up from 30% in Q4 2025), representing a durable new dimension to ASML's growth driven by AI accelerators requiring high bandwidth memory. Once memory makers commit to EUV intensive nodes, they keep buying tools for years, making this a sticky revenue stream rather than a one-time event.
buys.cautions: Primary risk is regulatory. ASML is already prohibited from selling its most advanced EUV chips to China, and the rules governing what it can sell and to whom are still evolving. China currently accounts for 20% of ASML's revenue, and any further tightening of restrictions or extensions of controls to additional product lines could trim that contribution further. Management acknowledged this directly, noting that 2026 revenue guidance range is set specifically to accommodate potential outcomes of ongoing discussions around export control. For a company with a hardware monopoly on the world's most critical technology, the risk isn't competition, it's policy, and the regulatory environment remains live and unpredictable.

buys.reason: The CFO Sheila Verma explained that Robinhood is transitioning from a pure trading platform to a financial super app with three distinct growth arcs: (1) becoming the #1 platform for active traders through continuous product innovation, (2) increasing wallet share by offering banking, credit cards, robo-advisory, and other financial products, and (3) building a global financial ecosystem across retail, B2B, and institutional clients. The company achieved 60+ billion in net deposits in 2024 with 35% annualized growth, and maintained 20% net deposit growth even during Q1 2025's difficult macro environment (government shutdown, software sell-off, global conflict). The addressable market is massive: $20 trillion in US retail brokerage, $20 trillion in US retirement, and $7-10 trillion in US advisory, with Robinhood at only $350 billion in assets under management. The company is gaining market share in options (recently #1), gaining in equities (#2 and compounding), and has new high-growth products (banking already at $2 billion deposits with 100,000+ customers; credit card at $10+ billion annualized GMV with 800,000 customers and millions on waitlist; prediction markets as fastest-growing product ever). Banking is macro-agnostic and direct deposit penetration is at healthy 40%. The company maintains profitable growth (revenues growing faster than expenses), is vertically integrating by owning prediction market exchange (Rhombus JV with Susquehanna), and is deploying aggressive buybacks ($1.5 billion program authorized, $300M+ executed in Q1 2025 alone) while still investing in organic growth and M&A. International expansion is beginning with UK, EU, Canada, Indonesia, and Singapore markets, with early UK cohorts looking similar to early US cohorts suggesting 10+ year runway for international growth.
buys.cautions: Execution risk is significant given the number of concurrent product launches and international expansions. Credit card delinquency and unit economics must be monitored carefully—the CFO emphasized deliberate and cautious rollout, comparing to Nubank and Upgrade (on pace) but noting Apple Card grew too fast and 'blew themselves up.' Banking and credit card products are still in early scaling phases and must achieve profitability at scale. Prediction markets pricing structure has been completely redesigned to be more competitive, which could impact near-term take rates. The company is dependent on continued net deposit growth and customer trust, which could be disrupted by market downturns or competitive threats. Regulatory risks exist especially internationally across multiple jurisdictions.

buys.reason: Rocket Lab is the only vertically integrated end-to-end space company that's publicly traded until SpaceX IPOs. The Electron rocket is the most frequently launched small rocket in the world with a 3D-printed Rutherford engine (first rocket engine with 3D-printed combustion chamber, injectors, pumps, and propellant valves) that cuts manufacturing time from months to days and dramatically reduces cost per engine. Earlier this month Rocket Lab reported their best quarter ever with $200 million in revenue, up over 60% year-over-year, and a backlog that hit $2.2 billion, up 20% quarter-over-quarter and 100% year-over-year. SpaceX is expected to IPO at roughly 60x forward revenue while Rocket Lab trades at 20x, suggesting Rocket Lab is relatively cheap even at an $84 billion market cap if SpaceX's valuation is justified. SpaceX is one of the first customers for Nvidia's Vera CPU, connecting the space and AI chip war narratives.

avoids.title: SpaceX: The Most Tragic IPO In Stock Market History
avoids.reason: SpaceX is priced at 94 times revenue, not earnings, which is almost 50% more expensive than Palantir (the highest price-to-sales ratio in the S&P 500 at 65x), while growing almost two times slower and with much lower margins. The company is not profitable. The valuation grew 12 times faster than revenue growth. 93% of SpaceX's total addressable market is AI (primarily the troubled xAI acquisition), where the company has minimal traction and must compete against entrenched players like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. The core space business (Falcon 9, Starship) is operating at a $657 million loss despite being 24 years old. Starship development lacks regulatory approval, passenger transport regulations, and has no timeline for commercial viability. The company also has severe governance issues: Elon Musk controls 85% of votes with only 42% of shares, has been granted $175 billion in performance-based shares that he can vote and use as collateral before vesting with no requirement to actually build a Mars colony, and has a corporate opportunity carve-out allowing him to take business opportunities elsewhere. Shareholders waive jury trial rights and have no real governance power.
avoids.risk: Significant downside risk given the 94x sales valuation, unprofitability, slowing revenue growth relative to valuation expansion, governance control concentrated in a single individual with conflicting incentives, and forced inclusion in the Nasdaq 100 index at only 4-5% free float, which will artificially inflate the stock price as passive investors are forced to buy.

Earnings have been declining for the last 4 years, revenue has been flat for years, and the EV business is seeing declines in both gross profit and revenue. The core issue is that Tesla is being valued not as a car company (which is 95% of its actual business today) but as a highly profitable software, robotics, AI, and energy company combined. This means investors are already pricing in massive future product successes across multiple categories that may never materialize or may be far less profitable than expected. Steve Eisman emphasizes that the EV market is not a good business as China produces EVs much cheaper than Tesla. If SpaceX acquires Tesla (as Eisman expects), it would be a value-destructive move for SpaceX shareholders.

buys.title: 2 Stocks the Entire AI Market Depends On !!
buys.reason: TSMC is the world's dominant contract chip maker and foundry that manufactures semiconductors for nearly every major fabless technology company including Nvidia, Apple, AMD, Broadcom, and Qualcomm. When it comes to the most advanced chips running AI training and inference at scale, TSMC holds something close to an absolute monopoly with its 3-nanometer and 2-nanometer process nodes being the cutting edge of what silicon can do today. AI has become the core of TSMC's business—the company's high-performance computing (AI accelerators, GPUs, and data center chips) increased 20% quarter over quarter to account for 61% of fourth quarter revenue. The company guided full-year 2026 revenue to grow above 30% in US dollar terms. TSMC is executing a coordinated multi-continent expansion of its 3 nanometer node with a new fab in Taiwan, a second Arizona fab, and Japan's second fab being upgraded to 3 nanometer. The CEO stated they are stepping up CapEx investments to increase N3 capacity, with the CFO noting that CapEx in the next three years will be significantly higher than the past three years (which totaled 101 billion), representing a capital investment cycle of a scale semiconductor manufacturing has never experienced. The shift from generative AI to agentic AI is driving a step up in token consumption and computation needs, with AI accelerator revenue now tracking towards a compounded annual growth rate in the high 50s, potentially nearly doubling in under two years.
buys.cautions: Geographic concentration risk—Taiwan is where the overwhelming majority of TSMC's most advanced manufacturing happens, making any geopolitical escalations involving Taiwan an existential risk to the global AI hardware supply chain and the stock. The Q1 2026 call also surfaced a near-term supply chain vulnerability with situations in the Middle East creating uncertainty around specialty chemicals and gases essential to chip fabrication, though management said they don't anticipate near-term disruption but acknowledged it was too early to quantify any potential impact on profitability.

buys.title: The Most Mispriced Stocks in the Market Right Now
buys.reason: The creator argues Uber is significantly undervalued despite being down 16.4% over 12 months and 28.8% from peak. The core thesis is that the market underestimates Uber's competitive moat and misunderstands its business scope. Waymo is valued at ~$13.3 billion in private markets, yet Uber trades at comparable valuation despite generating $53.6 billion in annual revenue, achieving 18.3% free cash flow margins, and being profitable—advantages Waymo does not possess. The creator dismisses the narrative that autonomous vehicles will be dominated by Tesla/Waymo (90%+ market share), arguing the market is large enough for multiple profitable players. Uber's current business is far from mature: mobility, delivery (huge contributor), commerce, and wholesale operations all have substantial runway. The company is positioned as a key distribution channel for autonomous vehicle fleets through its app ecosystem. Recent expansion into hotels (via Expedia partnership) demonstrates platform extension. Advertising and subscription businesses are growing rapidly. The company continues accelerating: 20.7% YoY growth last quarter, with expectations for a strong 2026 driven by World Cup tailwinds. US/Canada Uber operations are more lucrative than international, providing geographic upside. DCF valuation indicates fair value of $105 per share (47% upside from ~$71). Creator emphasizes Uber is generating billions in free cash flow annually while growing, yet market spends time on speculative names trading at 50-100x sales with unclear product adoption.
buys.when_to_buy: Current weakness presents buying opportunity
buys.target_price: $105 per share (47% upside based on DCF base case)
buys.cautions: Bull case assumes AV adoption accelerates and margins expand meaningfully as fleet deployment scales. Base case is described as 'very realistic.' Bear case assumes slower growth and lower margins, yielding $19.5% downside, though creator assigns only 20% probability to bear case and could reduce it to 10-15%, raising base case weighting. Full autonomous vehicle adoption could take many years, delaying margin expansion thesis. Competitive threats from Tesla and Waymo exist, though creator views these as manageable given market size. Current market skepticism suggests execution risk on strategic initiatives.

buys.title: Top Stocks I'm Buying For Huge Growth In July 2026
buys.reason: Every AI data center needs power and cooling. A single Nvidia rack today pulls around 120 kW (about 10 times more than traditional server racks), while Vera Rubin racks currently shipping take over 200 kW, with power requirements continuing to increase with each new generation. Nvidia partnered with Vertiv to redesign the entire power architecture from the ground up, moving data centers to 800 volts of DC power, the same standard Nvidia is building to eventually support 1 megawatt racks. Unlike most vendors who sell one piece of the puzzle, Vertiv sells both power and cooling as a single end-to-end architecture from the grid to the chip, all optimized to work together. Companies like Schneider, Eaton, and Delta are racing to the same 800V standard, but only Vertiv and Schneider deliver the full power and cooling stack as a single system. Vertiv also builds direct-to-chip liquid cooling. Every gigawatt-scale AI factory being built right now needs what Vertiv is selling, with orders growing by 250% year-over-year, the fastest growth in Vertiv's history.
